mini-gradebook-capstone.label: Final Project: Build a Mini Gradebook
mini-gradebook-capstone.summary: Finish the course with a longer gradebook capstone that grows step by step from basic report lines into a complete final summary.

capstone-mini-gradebook.title: Build the final gradebook report
capstone-mini-gradebook.prompt:
Start from the working gradebook preview you just built. Keep the loop, highest-score tracking, average calculation, and status logic, but replace the preview-style version with the final exact report.

Input format:
1. Student name
2. Number of scores
3. That many score values, one per line

Final required output:
Student: <name>
Scores entered: <count>
Highest score: <highest>
Average: <average>
Status: <status>
capstone-mini-gradebook.hint: The last step is a cleanup step: keep the calculations, remove the preview Total line, and print only the final report order.

help.concept: A smooth capstone ends by polishing a working program instead of starting over. Keep the same logic and shape the output into the final handoff format.
help.hint_1: Read the scores with `for i in range(count)`, update `total` and `highest`, then compute `average = total / count`.
help.hint_2: Print the final lines in the exact order shown, and choose the status with `if` / `elif` / `else`.

capstone-mini-gradebook.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

print(f"Student: {student}")
print(f"Scores entered: {count}")

total = 0
highest = 0
for i in range(count):
    score = int(input())
    total = total + score
    if score > highest:
        highest = score

average = total / count

print(f"Total: {total}")
print(f"Highest score: {highest}")
print(f"Average: {average:.1f}")

if average >= 90:
    print("Status: excellent")
elif average >= 70:
    print("Status: passing")
else:
    print("Status: needs practice")

# Replace the preview-style version with the final report.
# Remove the Total line and keep only the required final output order.

try-mini-gradebook-capstone-sketch0.title: Print the first gradebook line
try-mini-gradebook-capstone-sketch0.prompt: Start the capstone with one tiny report preview. Read a student name and print `Student: <name>`.
try-mini-gradebook-capstone-sketch0.hint: Use `input()` for the name, then print the labeled line with an f-string.

help.concept: The capstone grows one report line at a time, so the first preview only needs the student label.

try-mini-gradebook-capstone-sketch0.starterCode:
student = input()

# Print the first report line as Student: <name>

cp-1-read-gradebook-header.title: Read the gradebook header
cp-1-read-gradebook-header.prompt: A tutor wants the gradebook report to start with the student name and the score count. Read `student` and `count`, then print the first two report lines.
cp-1-read-gradebook-header.hint: This step only builds the report header: read the two inputs and label them clearly.

help.concept: A report often starts with identifying information before any calculations happen.

cp-1-read-gradebook-header.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

# Start the gradebook report.
# Print the student line and the scores-entered line.

cp-2-add-score-total.title: Add the score total
cp-2-add-score-total.prompt: Keep the working header lines. Now read exactly `count` scores with a `for` loop, add them into `total`, and print a preview line showing the total.
cp-2-add-score-total.hint: Let the loop do the repeated input work, and update `total` each time through.

help.concept: An accumulator starts at 0 and grows a little each time the loop reads a new value.
help.hint_2: Inside the loop, use `total = total + score`, then print `Total: ...` after the loop.

cp-2-add-score-total.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

print(f"Student: {student}")
print(f"Scores entered: {count}")

total = 0
for i in range(count):
    score = int(input())
    # Add each score to total.

# Print a preview line: Total: <total>

cp-3-track-highest-score.title: Track the highest score
cp-3-track-highest-score.prompt: Keep the header and total logic working. Now add `highest` so the program remembers the biggest score seen so far, then print that new preview line too.
cp-3-track-highest-score.hint: Only replace `highest` when the current score is bigger than the old one.

help.concept: A running maximum keeps the best value found so far as the loop moves through the input.
help.hint_2: Inside the loop, use `if score > highest:` before updating `highest`.

cp-3-track-highest-score.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

print(f"Student: {student}")
print(f"Scores entered: {count}")

total = 0
highest = 0
for i in range(count):
    score = int(input())
    total = total + score
    # Keep total working, then update highest when this score is bigger.

print(f"Total: {total}")
# Print Highest score: <highest>

cp-4-calculate-average.title: Calculate the average
cp-4-calculate-average.prompt: Keep the running total and highest score. After the loop, calculate the average and add the formatted average line to the preview report.
cp-4-calculate-average.hint: Do the division after the loop, then print the result with one digit after the decimal point.

help.concept: The loop collects the information first, and the average is computed once the total and count are both ready.
help.hint_1: Use `average = total / count` after the loop finishes.

cp-4-calculate-average.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

print(f"Student: {student}")
print(f"Scores entered: {count}")

total = 0
highest = 0
for i in range(count):
    score = int(input())
    total = total + score
    if score > highest:
        highest = score

print(f"Total: {total}")
print(f"Highest score: {highest}")

# Calculate average after the loop.
# Print Average: <average> with one digit after the decimal point.

cp-5-choose-status.title: Choose the status line
cp-5-choose-status.prompt: Keep the working preview report, then add the final status line: `excellent` for averages at least 90, `passing` for averages at least 70, and `needs practice` otherwise.
cp-5-choose-status.hint: Use an `if` / `elif` / `else` chain after the average is ready.

help.concept: Once a program has the final score summary, conditionals can turn it into a simple human-friendly judgment.
help.hint_2: Use `elif average >= 70` for the middle case, and `else` for the remaining case.

cp-5-choose-status.starterCode:
student = input()
count = int(input())

print(f"Student: {student}")
print(f"Scores entered: {count}")

total = 0
highest = 0
for i in range(count):
    score = int(input())
    total = total + score
    if score > highest:
        highest = score

average = total / count

print(f"Total: {total}")
print(f"Highest score: {highest}")
print(f"Average: {average:.1f}")

# Add the status line.
# Print excellent for averages 90+, passing for 70+, and needs practice otherwise.

try_mini_gradebook_capstone_sketch0.title: Try it yourself
try_mini_gradebook_capstone_sketch0.prompt: Read one student name and print the first report line as `Student: <name>`.

sketch_gradebook_plan.title: Plan the gradebook
sketch_gradebook_plan.bodyMarkdown:
Imagine a tutor getting ready for parent check-ins. She does not need a giant school system yet. She needs one dependable Python program that can read a student name, collect scores, and turn them into a report she can read quickly.

This capstone now grows in a clear sequence: first the header, then the total, then the highest score, then the average, then the status, and finally the polished final report. Each step starts from the previous working version so the whole project feels like one small real tool instead of one giant jump.
